summary:
Proximal surface divergence causes natural embrasures that tend to occur lingually due to inherent tooth crown inclinations; these are most pronounced in molar regions where such anatomical features lead to greater spacing specifically towards the tongue-side. Agent1 reasoned that since dental crowns usually lean slightly toward the backside (tongue), if the sides don't meet precisely at contacts, it creates an opening primarily behind each tooth rather than beside them. This summary captures both why divergences matter clinically—because they affect how well teeth fit together—and highlights which direction those openings commonly appear based on typical tooth positioning.

Agent1 reasoned that since dental crowns usually lean slightly toward the backside (tongue), if the sides don't meet precisely at contacts, it creates an opening primarily behind each tooth rather than beside them. This summary captures both why divergences matter clinically—because they affect how well teeth fit together—and highlights which direction those openings commonly appear based on typical tooth positioning.
